text: In Chinese, '我' means {{c1::I / me}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǒ}}.
explanation: '我' (Wǒ) is the first-person singular pronoun in Chinese. Unlike English, Chinese uses the same word for both subject ('I') and object ('me').
usage:
Term: 我 (Wǒ)
  • 我是学生。 — I am a student.
  • 他认识我。 — He knows me.
spanish: En chino, '我' significa 'Yo / Me'. Pronunciación: Wǒ.

text: In Chinese, '你' means {{c1::you (singular)}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐ}}.
explanation: '你' (Nǐ) is the second-person singular pronoun. A respectful/formal version is '您' (Nín), used when addressing elders or superiors.
usage:
Term: 你 (Nǐ)
  • 你是谁? — Who are you?
  • 我爱你。 — I love you.
spanish: En chino, '你' significa 'Tú / Usted'. Pronunciación: Nǐ.

text: In spoken Chinese, the pronoun for He, She, and It all sound the same: {{c1::tā}}. The characters differ: He=他, She=她, It=它.
explanation: In written Chinese, '他' (he), '她' (she), and '它' (it) are distinct. But in spoken Mandarin, all three are pronounced identically as 'tā'. Context determines meaning.
usage:
Term: 他/她/它 (tā)
  • 她是我的老师。 — She is my teacher.
  • 他很忙。 — He is very busy.
spanish: En chino, 他/她/它 todos suenan igual: 'tā' (él/ella/ello). Los caracteres son distintos.

text: In Chinese, '我们' means {{c1::We / us}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǒmen}}.
explanation: '我们' (Wǒmen) is the first-person plural pronoun. The suffix '们' (men) is added to singular pronouns to make them plural: 你们 (you all), 他们 (they).
usage:
Term: 我们 (Wǒmen)
  • 我们是朋友。 — We are friends.
  • 我们一起去吧! — Let's go together!
spanish: En chino, '我们' significa 'Nosotros'. Pronunciación: Wǒmen.

text: In Chinese, '他们' means {{c1::They}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Tāmen}}.
explanation: '他们' (Tāmen) is the third-person plural. For an all-female group '她们' is used, though '他们' is gender-neutral in plural.
usage:
Term: 他们 (Tāmen)
  • 他们在哪里? — Where are they?
  • 他们都是工程师。 — They are all engineers.
spanish: En chino, '他们' significa 'Ellos/Ellas'. Pronunciación: Tāmen.

text: In Chinese, the verb 'to be' (for identity/classification) is {{c1::是 (shì)}}. Example: 我是老师 = {{c2::I am a teacher}}.
explanation: '是' (shì) is used to link a subject with a noun (identity). Unlike English 'be', it is NOT used with adjectives. For adjectives, Chinese omits the verb entirely: 她很美 = She is beautiful.
usage:
Term: 是 (shì)
  • 这是我的手机。 — This is my phone.
  • 他是墨西哥人。 — He is Mexican.
spanish: En chino, 'ser/estar' con sustantivos se dice '是 (shì)'. No se usa con adjetivos.

text: In Chinese, '不是' means {{c1::am not / is not / are not}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Bú shì}}.
explanation: Negation in Chinese is formed by placing '不' (bù/bú) before the verb. '不是' (bú shì) negates '是'. Note the tone change: 不 changes from 4th to 2nd tone before another 4th tone.
usage:
Term: 不是 (Bú shì)
  • 我不是你的老师。 — I am not your teacher.
  • 这不是我的书包。 — This is not my backpack.
spanish: En chino, '不是 (bú shì)' significa 'no ser'. La negación se forma con '不' antes del verbo.

text: To turn any statement into a yes/no question in Chinese, add {{c1::吗 (ma)}} at the end. Example: 你是学生吗? = {{c2::Are you a student?}}
explanation: '吗' (ma) is a question particle that converts statements to yes/no questions with no word order change. This makes Chinese question formation simpler than in English.
usage:
Term: 吗 (ma)
  • 你饿吗? — Are you hungry?
  • 他是你朋友吗? — Is he your friend?
spanish: En chino, añadir '吗 (ma)' al final de una oración la convierte en pregunta de sí/no.

text: In Chinese, the possessive particle is {{c1::的 (de)}}. Example: 我的书 = {{c2::My book}}.
explanation: '的' (de) works like the English apostrophe 's. It follows a pronoun or noun to show possession. 你的 = your, 他的 = his, 我们的 = our.
usage:
Term: 的 (de)
  • 这是我的电脑。 — This is my computer.
  • 他们的办公室在三楼。 — Their office is on the third floor.
spanish: En chino, '的 (de)' funciona como el apóstrofe 's para indicar posesión. 我的 = mío, 你的 = tuyo.

text: In Chinese, '你们' means {{c1::You all / You (plural)}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Nǐmen}}.
explanation: '你们' is the informal second-person plural. The formal plural '您们' (Nínmen) exists but is rarely used — in formal settings, addressing a group, '各位' (gèwèi = everyone) is preferred.
usage:
Term: 你们 (Nǐmen)
  • 你们好! — Hello everyone! (group greeting)
  • 你们准备好了吗? — Are you all ready?
spanish: En chino, '你们' significa 'Vosotros / Ustedes'. Pronunciación: Nǐmen.

scenario: Chinese A1: There Is / There Are — 有 🧩
text: In Chinese, '有' (yǒu) expresses {{c1::existence / there is / to have}}. Example: 这里有一家咖啡店 = {{c2::There is a coffee shop here}}.
explanation: '有' (yǒu) serves as both 'to have' and 'there is/are'. Its negative is '没有' (méiyǒu) — does not have / there is not.
usage:
Term: 有 (yǒu)
  • 我有一个问题。 — I have a question.
  • 这里没有信号。 — There is no signal here.
spanish: En chino, '有 (yǒu)' significa 'tener / haber'. Su negativo es '没有 (méiyǒu)'.

text: In Chinese, '在' (zài) expresses {{c1::location / to be at / to be in}}. Example: 我在北京 = {{c2::I am in Beijing}}.
explanation: '在' (zài) is used instead of '是' when talking about location. It means 'to be at/in' a place. Note: 我是北京 is grammatically wrong for location.
usage:
Term: 在 (zài)
  • 她在图书馆。 — She is at the library.
  • 文件在桌子上。 — The file is on the desk.
spanish: En chino, '在 (zài)' indica ubicación: 'estar en un lugar'. Se usa en lugar de '是' para expresar dónde está algo.

text: In Chinese, '我叫...' means {{c1::My name is... / I am called...}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Wǒ jiào...}}
explanation: '叫' (jiào) literally means 'to be called'. '我叫Jesus' — I am called Jesus. You can also say '我的名字是Jesus' (Wǒ de míngzi shì Jesus), which uses '是'.
usage:
Term: 我叫 (Wǒ jiào)
  • 大家好，我叫 Jesus。 — Hello everyone, my name is Jesus.
spanish: En chino, '我叫...' significa 'Me llamo...'. Pronunciación: Wǒ jiào.

text: In Chinese, adjective sentences require the intensifier {{c1::很 (hěn)}} before the adjective. Example: 她很忙 = {{c2::She is very busy}}.
explanation: In Chinese, when an adjective is used as the predicate, '很' must be placed before it. While '很' literally means 'very', in simple statements it acts as a linking word and its intensity is often neutral.
usage:
Term: 很 (hěn)
  • 他很高。 — He is tall.
  • 中文很难。 — Chinese is difficult.
spanish: En chino, '很 (hěn)' es necesario entre el sujeto y el adjetivo predicativo. Técnicamente significa 'muy'.

text: In Chinese, '也' means {{c1::also / too / as well}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Yě}}.
explanation: '也' (yě) is placed after the subject and before the verb, not at the end of a sentence like in English. Position matters: 我也是 = I am also / Me too.
usage:
Term: 也 (yě)
  • A: 我是工程师。— B: 我也是。(Me too / I'm also [one].)
  • 她也学中文。 — She also studies Chinese.
spanish: En chino, '也 (yě)' significa 'también'. Va después del sujeto y antes del verbo.

text: In Chinese, '这' means {{c1::this}} (Zhè) and '那' means {{c2::that}} (Nà).
explanation: '这' (zhè) and '那' (nà) are demonstrative pronouns/adjectives. Add '个' (ge) for 'this one / that one': 这个 / 那个. They are used with '是' to identify things.
usage:
Term: 这 (zhè) / 那 (nà)
  • 这是什么? — What is this?
  • 那个人是谁? — Who is that person?
spanish: En chino, '这 (zhè)' = este/esta y '那 (nà)' = ese/aquel.

text: In Chinese, '谁' means {{c1::Who}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Shéi / Shuí}}.
explanation: '谁' (shéi or shuí, both pronunciations are correct) is the question word for 'who'. Chinese question words stay in the same position as the answer would be.
usage:
Term: 谁 (Shéi)
  • 你是谁? — Who are you?
  • 谁是你的老板? — Who is your boss?
spanish: En chino, '谁 (shéi)' significa '¿Quién?'. Las palabras interrogativas mantienen su posición en la oración.

text: In Chinese, '什么' means {{c1::What}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Shénme}}.
explanation: '什么' (shénme) is the most common question word. Like other Chinese question words, it stays in the same position as the answer: 你叫什么名字? (You called what name?)
usage:
Term: 什么 (Shénme)
  • 这是什么? — What is this?
  • 你要什么? — What do you want?
spanish: En chino, '什么 (shénme)' significa '¿Qué?'. Permanece en la posición de la respuesta.

scenario: Chinese A1: Numbers — One to Five 🧩
text: In Chinese, the numbers 1-5 are: 一(yī), 二(èr), 三(sān), 四(sì), 五(wǔ). What is {{c1::三 (sān)}} and {{c2::五 (wǔ)}} in English?
explanation: Chinese numerals are phonetically simple and consistent. 一=1, 二=2, 三=3, 四=4, 五=5. Numbers are essential for counting, phone numbers, prices, and telling time.
usage:
Term: 一二三四五 (yī èr sān sì wǔ)
  • 我要三个包子。 — I want three buns.
  • 我们有五个人。 — We have five people.
spanish: En chino: 一(yī)=1, 二(èr)=2, 三(sān)=3, 四(sì)=4, 五(wǔ)=5.

scenario: Chinese A1: Not Have — 没有 🧩
text: In Chinese, '没有' means {{c1::don't have / there is not}}. Pronunciation: {{c2::Méiyǒu}}.
explanation: '没有' (méiyǒu) is the negation of '有' (to have). It is also used like 'there is not': 没有人 = There is nobody. In everyday speech, '没有' is often shortened to '没' (méi).
usage:
Term: 没有 (Méiyǒu)
  • 我没有钱。 — I don't have money.
  • 没有问题! — No problem!
spanish: En chino, '没有 (méiyǒu)' significa 'no tener / no haber'. Es la negación de '有'.
